replies.title: Re: The destruction of Atlantis
replies.content:
1) It was ten thousand years ago and all we've got are dim folk memories, a few very equivocal archeological traces, and the fact that the oceans rose sharply at the right time. The occult tradition has it that Atlantis fell because its leaders fell under the sway of evil powers; what that means in terms of politics, culture, technology, and so on is probably not something we'll ever know.

2) The programs Dion Fortune wrote about, the ones that left such extensive traces in Celtic societies (due to a lot of survivors reaching northwest Europe during and after the period of acute sea level rise), were Atlantean.

3) The Picatrix isn't something to handle lightly. It includes quite a bit of powerful and dangerous stuff, and some of the practices are boobytrapped -- Chris and I did our best to identify the traps and warn people, but it requires a great deal of care and thorough knowledge to use effectively. What you sensed from the diagrams may simply have been a "do not touch this!" warning from your intuition.

replies.title: Re: The destruction of Atlantis
replies.content:
(Different Anonymous)

If Atlantis was very advanced technologically speaking and didn't use oil (or at least didn't manage to blow through the entire world's oil supply that was created over hundreds of millions of years over 200 years), why does peak oil necessitate an irreversible decline in civilization for us?

replies.title: Re: The destruction of Atlantis
replies.content:
Because we've become so dependent on it. If we'd followed the good advice we received in the 1970s, and began retooling our technology for sustainability when we were still using only a fraction of the fossil fuels we use now, we could have made a transition to a steady state technology that would have been viable for many centuries. We didn't. Instead, we made our technology and infrastructure ever more dependent on larger and larger inputs of fossil fuel. Now that it's depleting fast, we no longer have the energy supply or the resources to meet the costs of transition, so down we go.

Think of it this way. If you inherited ten million dollars and were smart, you'd invest it and live off the interest without touching those ten million dollars, and you'd be set for life. If you weren't smart, you'd say, "Whee, ten million dollars!" and start spending it, and before you know it you'd be on the brink of running out and being poor again. Fossil fuels were our ten million dollar investment, and we weren't smart...

comments.title: Regarding the increased use of hidden potential
comments.content:
You had mentioned on the other blog, that the human mind is capable of much more than we would ordinarily like to think it is, but WITHIN REASONABLE RESTRICTIONS. Thus, as an engineer on the mathematical side of things, I can be much more successful at building mathematical models by training my mind; however, I'm well aware that no amount of mental training will make me as capable as a mathematical genius or someone who is child prodigy like David Hilbert, Terence Tao, etc.

That said, I would like to train my mind to boost my capabilities within the reasonable limits I mentioned above. If I remember correctly, you said that there are specific methods that enable this, one of which is discursive meditation. Could you list some others? Also, could you suggest a book or any other resource for this kind of mental training, which employs the methods used by occultists to do mental training?

replies.title: Re: Regarding the increased use of hidden potential
replies.content:
Discursive meditation is central to any form of mind training; also, concentration exercises are essential; Mouni Sadhu's book Concentration is a good source here. Beyond that, though, methods of training the mind depend on what you intend to do, and teaching yourself to build mathematical models isn't something occultists have put much time into, since mathematical models aren't especially useful to us!

Occult training of the mind tends to focus on developing psychism (paranormal abilities), developing intuition, and developing memory. For the first, W.E. Butler's How to See the Aura, Practice Psychometry, Telepathy, and Clairvoyance is the text I recommend. For the second, daily practice of divination using any oracle you like is the best way. For the third, Frances Yates' The Art of Memory is a good starting place.

replies.title: Re: Regarding the increased use of hidden potential
replies.content:
Not really occult, but if would like a few more options, here are some to consider:

1) The "Psmiths" write very interesting book reviews, and the husband of the duo is pretty into math, so many of his reviews touch on it from a more intuitive, deep-understanding way. The latest by him touches on the efforts of an early "AI" researcher to teach kids math using a computer programming language, but along the way he links to other relevant reviews and includes a discussion on how much of "intelligence" is innate versus can be developed and so forth: https://www.thepsmiths.com/p/review-mindstorms-by-seymour-papert

2) Mind Performance Hacks and Mindhacker by Ron and Marty Hale-Evans are collections of techniques for doing various cognitive tasks better, from memory to coming up with novel ideas to focus and more. Their stated goal is to come up with the skills to "be a mentat," though there's less focus in these books on raw calculation than Dune implies for the mentat.

3) Though they tend to be less focused on mathematical models specifically, there's a whole literature on "mental models" for improved clarity in thinking and decision-making. The concept was at least partially pioneered and popularized by Charlie Munger, Warren Buffett's business partner, so often the presentation of such assumes you're looking for useful ways of thinking about business, but many are generally useful. The blog Farnam Street has done a lot on them and has published a series of lovely books explaining the most useful ones.

4) The short essay "A Mathematician's Lament" by Paul Lockhart was written by a research mathematician turned math teacher about how the way our culture teaches math stifles all of what is beautiful and interesting about math, and proposes some better approaches. Lockhart has also written some other books on mathematical concepts, like Arithmetic and Measurement, which I understand as trying to get at what these fields of math are doing at a foundational level, but in a way understandable to non-specialists, but I haven't read them yet, so I can't speak to how useful they might be.

5) Though he went publicly dark a while back, Eric Weinstein (brother of his by-now better known brother Bret Weinstein) is a mathematician by training and thinks he has come up with a "theory of everything" that solves various inconsistencies/problems with the mathematical models of fundamental physics. I've listened to some of his talks on the subject, but they went waaaay over my head. I mention him because he has said that his way of approaching math and thinking about it is very different from almost every other serious mathematician he's met, so perhaps his explanations or descriptions of intuitions could spark some novel approaches.

6) Rather tangentially related, but The Art of Learning by Josh Waitzkin is a lovely book on the process of learning a field deeply, drawn from his experiences as first a youth chess champion, and then a world champion at Tai Qi Quan Push Hands. It's not about math, but he tries to derive principles about learning that apply across fields. It's very abstractness might be a help in this case.

Hope these help, and good luck in your pursuits!
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Regarding the increased use of hidden potential
replies.content: Not sure what your math background is but I think that trying to wrap your head around something like "Introductory Real Analysis" by Kolmogrov is a good way to go beyond standard engineering math and into some more abstract areas.  It's available as a cheap Dover addition so it won't break the bank like some math texts.

comments.title: Blessings; Hermetic initiation ritual in Manly P. Hall?
comments.content:
Good morning JMG,

And I hope everybody had a good weekend! :-)

1. To everybody:

I‘m offering blessings again on Wednesday, for everybody who signs up. More info and signup here:

https://thehiddenthings.com/weekly-blessings-14

I practice my blessing skills, and you‘d do me a favour by signing up! :-)

2. To JMG, and everybody else who has pointers for me:

In Manly P. Hall‘s „Secret Teachings of all Ages“, at the beginning of his chapter about Hermes Trismegistos, he gives a curious and dramatic account of a mystery initiation ritual in a temple:

Thunder rolling, the veil of the temple, hung between two lotus-headed columns, tears. The initiator does/says dramatic stuff, the candidate trembles. From the darkness appears a luminous haze/cloud, Thoth Hermes Thrice Greatest appears, surrounded by greenish sheen. His heart changes into an ibis, his brain into a flashing emerald. He holds the caduceus with live snakes.

Then Typhon appears. Hermes hits him with his staff. Typhon dies, and Hermes puts his foot upon Typhon‘s head. Then Hermes disappears again with a bang.

I‘m almost certain that Hall didn’t make this story up himself, but got it from another source, and (probably) paraphrased it.

A. Do you have any idea where he might have taken the account of the Hermetic mystery initiation from?

B. There are some stories about Hermes engaging Typhon, but as far as I can tell, only in the context of Zeus‘ fight with him. And Hermes always seems to do Hermes things (stealing from Typhon, tricking him). I can‘t find an account of Hermes engaging Typhon in a direct fight.

Is anybody aware of any such account?

(It’s mostly the mystery initiation account from A. I’m looking for, and I‘m hoping that B. might be a clue towards finding A.)

C. Do you know any general material about/accounts of Hermetic (= Hermes-themed) Mystery Initiation rituals?

I‘m mostly thinking about ancient temple rituals, but maybe there are newer practices as well (i.e. accounts of such rituals, mentions of them in other texts, but also „scientific“ studies and investigations).

Thanks a lot in advance to everybody who might have pointers for me, and especially to JMG for providing this wonderful and interesting space again!

Milkyway

replies.title: Re: Blessings; Hermetic initiation ritual in Manly P. Hall?
replies.content: Hall didn't get the material in that book from any source. He wrote it all in a state of intense inspiration. Asked about the sources afterwards by occultists, he'd say, "sometimes I know things, but I don't know why I know them." So you now know as much as anyone about his sources! As for the old mystery initiation rituals, they remain as secret as they were in ancient times. There are several decent books out there that cover the tiny scraps that are known; Marvin Meyer's anthology The Ancient Mysteries is especially good.

replies.title: Re: Blessings; Hermetic initiation ritual in Manly P. Hall?
replies.content:
Unfortunately, my copy is at my office at work, so I can't confirm, but I think there might be something about Hermes fighting Typhon in the Homeric Hymns, but it's possible I'm mixing that up with Apollo and Python. After the heavy hitters Homer and Hesiod, these are some of our best early primary sources for the Greek myths.

For a general reference work, Edith Hamilton's Mythology is very good (unfortunately also at work), and if you have access to a fairly nice university research library, you might be able to check out the Oxford Classical Dictionary, either physically or online (https://oxfordre.com/classics) - some entries are public, but most are restricted, including the one on Typhon.

Good luck tracking these down!
Jeff

comments.title: Wounds to physical and etheric bodies
comments.content:
Hi JMG, thanks for this MM as always.

Strange question here. Several years ago, I gave the top of my head a good whack on a piece of stiff timber while not paying close attention under a low roof.

The area bled a little bit, healed up so I thought, but then a few months later I noticed that the hair in that part of the scalp started to go thin. It's hard to see unless you're looking for it, but there's a small oval patch where the hair doesn't grow quite as it should.

Ever since then, in quiet moments when my mind's open to seeing the invisible, I've experienced the impression of a sharp, jagged object, like a chunk of iron or even black glass, embedded in that spot.

My training in magic and such matters is minimal, but I do have a background with meditation and visualization. On a few occasions I've visualized myself pulling out the intruding object, which is followed by a sensation of warmth and relief. The overall feeling is not unlike pulling a physical splinter out of the material body, though this is a much larger "object".

It seems odd to me that the symptoms of this otherwise boring injury have carried on for so long, and also that I'm experiencing these images.

Do physical wounds carry over to the etheric or astral bodies? Is there anything else that could be going on?

Much thanks.

Dear John, I’m X once again happy to ask you my questions this Magic Monday.

1. In your geomancy book, the topic of Impedition considers an example of a woman interested in starting a sexual relationship with one of her coworkers and her Mothers are: Amissio, Amissio, Carcer and Fortuna Minor. The querent significator in the 5th house was Via - no perfection - but the judge and the right witness (querent) were both Acquisitio while the left witness (quesited) was populus. Since there was not a direct perfection, it was concluded that the relationship was not going to happen. But the judge and the RW were both Acquisitio, a powerful figure of ‘yesses’. How is it that the lack of perfection overruled the judge and court’s verdict?

Also there was another perfection by translation between the H1 and H10 - her place of work. Does that mean that while her target person might not start a relationship with her, another person at work will?

2. I know you said that you don't work with the grimoires but I wish to ask about the Heptameron whose conjurations have made it into your geomancy book. Do you use that conjuration as well as its sigil/seal or do you use the Rose cross sigil of the archangels?

In the Heptameron, the archangel is called last preceded by the heavenly angel (Tetra for Mercury) preceded by the godnames. But neither the golden dawn or Agrippa's 3 books have a mention of the heavenly angels, which also didn't make it to the Golden Dawn.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Because that was an example for instructional purposes, not an actual divination.

2) When I say I don't work with the grimoires, that really does mean I don't work with the grimoires. Borrowing some prayers from the Heptameron doesn't change that. They were simply convenient prayers.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1. Yes, you mention that the judge and right witness are positive but that also doesn't mean anything because of the denial. However, for my own learning's sake, let's say that example was real and there was a perfection by translation between the H1 and H10 - her place of work. Does that mean that while her target person might not start a relationship with her, another person at work will?

2. I see. There are some angelic grimoires so basic that they contain just a prayer and a conjuration call to the spirit, like the Arbatel. The Heptameron is also simplistic and requires no triangle, wand, crystal, Holy Table or anything. Some would say that just using its main conjuration as well as the sigil counts as working with the grimoires.
I myself am tempted to use the Heptameron instead of Circle's conjurations which don't make use of the same godnames.

3. Can you tell me what is the difference between conjurations from one grimoire vs another? Both are just an invitation/call to the angel/spirit making use of different godnames. The Heptameron prayer calls more than just the archangel. It calls the heavenly angel first, archangel last.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) The usual reason for failure in geomantic divination is overthinking the chart, and trying to read more into it than is there. This is a good example. A chart answers the question you ask, not any question you can think of applying to it afterwards.

3) I literally have no idea what you're trying to ask here. The difference between conjurations in one grimoire and another is of course that they're in different grimoires, therefore from different traditions of practice. I assume you get that, though.

If someone were to not follow the grimoires, like you, but seek to attain the same results as purported by the grimoires, how would one go about it?
Let's take the example of something similar to the first pentacle of the Moon, but keeping it to our Golden Dawn style.

The point is, 'for the purposes of opening doors in the life of the magician', something the Moon is attributed to as well as Mercury.


How would one go about it?

I'm having difficulty with performing golden dawn operations for results purported in the KoS and the Picatrix.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
It's important to understand that the Golden Dawn is a whole system -- you can't simply do bits and pieces out of it. If you want to master GD magic and get its benefits, you need to set aside time for daily practice -- LBRP and Middle Pillar, discursive meditation, and divination -- and yes, that's every single day, preferably first thing after getting up. Another couple of hours a week for study, and then an hour or so a week for working major rituals. That does a lot of the work of transforming your life all by itself, since it builds will and imagination, which are the keys to success in life as well as in magic.

In the process, you forget about what the grimoires say. The GD is a whole system and it has its own rules. You don't just "open doors in the life of the magician" -- some of those doors lead to things you don't want. You choose what you want, figure out what element or planet or sephirah corresponds to it, and do workings accordingly. Beyond that -- why, you follow the instructions, in my books or those of other GD magic. Follow them to the letter without quibbling, btw; that's good training for the will. And then, the results follow.

I got the idea partly from my own meditations and partly from my study of Transactional Analysis, a school of psychology that was popular in the 1970s but got swept under the rug once therapists stopped curing mental problems and started drugging people into numbness instead. You can find a fair amount about TA online and in older libraries.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: That's fascinating. I remember reading Eric Berne's books fifty years ago, but had completely forgotten them. I will follow that idea up. If I may ask a supplementary question, then meditating on this issue for some time now, I have repeatedly come up against the belief that what I recognise as handicaps and obstacles in this life are attempts to ensure that I do not repeat, or punish myself for, things that I did in previous lives. (I am not confusing this with the actions of karma.) Thus, an aversion all my life to seeking or accepting positions of responsibility I'd actually like to have had is apparently linked to the belief that I had such a position in an earlier life and misused it, causing suffering to others along the way. I am thinking my way through this, but I wondered if the concept was one you were familiar with?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Yes, and there are two sides to it. On the one hand, yes, this is a thing that happens, so that you can recover from some serious mistakes and prepare to do better. On the other, sooner or later you're going to end up in a position of responsibility and face the temptation to misuse it. If you can refuse the temptation and do the right thing instead, you've finally resolved all the karma from your previous error and can go on.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hi JMG,
What a fantastic research and translation job you and Warnock did. Congratulations! Could you comment more on accessing the primary sources (which was probably difficult) and how the process of translating the Spanish and Latin of the time into today's English was like (I imagine the languages ​​were mixed in the same sentence).

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: We didn't have to go chasing after primary sources, since David Pingree published a critical edition of the Latin text many years ago. As for the language, it was Latin vocabulary but Spanish word order, the sort of thing that was common in the Middle Ages; once we got the hang of it, it was mostly a matter of getting used to medieval magical and astrological vocabulary.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Alfonso the Wise's translatiors translated the Arabic text of the Picatrix into Spanish first, and then somone translated that Spanish version -- now lost, except for fragments -- into the Latin text that we now have (and that Pingree edited). Pingree discusses this in his article "Between the Ghāya and Picatrix, I: the Spanish Version," in the Journal of the Warburg and Courtauld Institutes, 44 (1981), 27-56.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Yes, I recall that -- Chris and I read a lot of Pingree in the process. Good Latinists even in Alfonso el Sabio's time could take a text in the vulgar, though, and put it into fairly elegant Latin; this was the kind of dog-Latin you expect from someone who is translating word by word rather than sentence by sentence.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Yes indeed.  And that is why the Latin translation of the Spanish translation may not have been made in Alfonso's court, but elsewhere, for the use of non-Spaniards.

comments.title: Cosmogenesis etc
comments.content:
I have immensely enjoyed recently discovering your writings and interviews, and you have given me much new food for thought with your wide ranging knowledge and wisdom. I gather that although you have been critical of Blavatsky and Theosophy, your beliefs in many ways correspond or at least converge with theirs: for example, karma/action, reincarnation, the afterlife sojourn, the manifold planes (e.g. upper triad and personality/lower vehicles) of the human and cosmic constititions, the monad's long progressive journey through the kingdoms of nature (mineral, plant, animal, human, beyond...mastership?), a multitude of evolved but finite gods and skepticism about a monotheistic infinite/supreme being, etc.

If humans eventually evolve/graduate into the next kindgom, is this kingdom comprised of the equivalent of your kingdom of polytheistic gods (or at least the higher portions of it)? And how does it compare with Blavatsky's "fifth kingdom" (kingdom of gods/heaven)? Relatedly, who do you believe Blavatsky's "mahatmas" truly were...genuine spiritual masters of this fifth kingdom (the next step on the evolutionary ladder), discarnate errant or deluded/limited (but sincere and honest) philosophers, advanced demons with an insidious geopolitical agenda, her unwitting or unconscious idealizations of people she actually knew, honest hallucinations/projections by Blavatasky, or intentional inventions by a charlatan/fraudster extraordinaire (HPB)?

Is human civilization actually trillions of years old, and is there any empirical evidence for the apparently infinite cyclical cosmogenesis/anthropogenesis propounded in theosophy (as well as in some forms of Hinduism and Buddhism such as the puranas, vedas, pali cannon, though using different language/terminology)? Are such theosophical ideas (regardless of their true origins) worth knowing, understanding, integrating in life? Do you believe there is really an isolable body of beliefs that could be fairly termed the "Ageless or Perennial Wisdom Teachings" that form the fount or source of all the great religions, or is this just a false "umbrella term" to lend legitimacy or credibility to otherwise dubious geo-history and faux spirituality?

I am a bit familiar with the Coloumb inquiry and have read conflicting interpretations (difficult to find neutral/objective info on this contested history); also read that the originals Mahatma Letters have been examined by independent commisions of experts who have concluded that they were indeed not written/forged by Blavatasky. Also, some of her critics seem to have ignored the plausible evidence that her "adepts" did also exist in physical form as actual persons.

Given all the above, what should we take (if anything) from the originary corpus of theosophical teachings (notwithstanding its many new age offshoots and subsequent distortions)? What is worth salvaging and continuing to build upon and what is plainly false and should be discarded? And how to your mind did this split, advancing both truth and error, attain and perdure? I realize no tradition or body of knowledge is absolute, infallible, or all-encompassing, but am trying to separate the wheat from the shaff...or avoid the common (black/white, either/or, binary) temptation to discard the baby with the bathwater.

If you could you help me (begin to) sort these things out I would be very grateful. I feel as though I'm starting all over at Square One with my spiritual/religious beliefs...a mental maelstrom/dark night! Feel free to also suggest your books or links to blogs/interviews where you address some of these ideas and questions. I truly appreciate your widsom and intellectual honesty. PS My deepest condolences on the loss of your wife Sara.

Anthony

replies.title: Re: Cosmogenesis etc
replies.content: This is a question for a book, not a brief response! There are good reasons to think that Blavatsky had access to a genuine body of espteric tradition, and I find K. Paul Johnson's identification of them with living people (which Blavatsky always said they were) tolerably convincing. It's also clear that she filled out the gaps in what she received with a free mix of 19th century science, alternative spiritual teachings, and personal visionary experiences. Thus Blavatsky's writings are a mixed bag; they're worth studying but should never be believed blindly as simple objective fact. The best approach to them, it seems to me, is the one Dion Fortune (who studied HPB's work very closely, and respected it) proposed to readers of her book The Cosmic Doctrine: "In these occult teachings you will be given certain images, under which you are instructed to think of certain things. These images are not descriptive but symbolic, and are designed to to train the mind, not to inform it."

replies.title: Re: Cosmogenesis etc
replies.content:
What our host said: K. Paul Johnson's identification of the real, living people behind Blavatsky's mahatmas is quite convincing, and HPB herself is worthy of respect as an occultist. That said, she was not infallible (and never claimed to be, of course), even though many 20th-century theosophists seem to say, "Blavatsky said it, I believe it, that settles it!" My own first esoteric teacher (outside of my own family), Clesson Hopkins Harvey, was of that school of theosophical thought.

Also, Blavatsky showed herself fairly knowledgeable about the arts of deception, as quite a number of worth-while occultists have been. This emphatically does NOT make them less worthy of respect. They are out to "train the mind, not inform it," and exposure to deceit is an essential part of any really good training of the mind. The morality or immorality of deception is entirely beside the point in such training.

replies.title: Re: Cosmogenesis etc
replies.content: Thank you. Sounds similar to the mystical Christian/Catholic notion of discernment.

replies.title: Re: Cosmogenesis etc
replies.content: Thank you! May I just follow up by parsing a basic question from my original comment?: What/who is your "5th kingdom" comprised of...and do Blavatsky's mahatmas qualify in your mind?

replies.title: Re: Cosmogenesis etc
replies.content: From my perspective humans aren't a separate kingdom, we're simply one expression of the transitional stage between the animal realm and the next realm up, which is that of beings who no longer need material bodies (their densest body is on the etheric plane). All beings in our swarm of souls start out on the mineral level, work up through the plant and animal levels, and then pass on to the next realm. That higher realm -- call it the realm of spirits if you like -- is as diverse as the mineral, plant, and animal realms; just as the animal kingdom includes everything from paramecia to blue whales, the spirit kingdom includes an equal range of complexity -- and yes, humans who "graduate" from our transitional status are in the paramecium category. (Furthermore, there are levels above that one, which we can't even imagine yet; we'll get there in due time.) Blavatsky was very specific that her mahatmas were incarnate human beings, so they didn't belong to the spirit realm (though they may have gotten there since her time).

replies.title: Re: Cosmogenesis etc
replies.content: I wonder though if her mahatmas took physical bodies voluntarily (self-fashioned "mayavarupas", post graduation from the human realm) or out of their evolutionary/karmic necessity (and possibly on the cusp of the spiritual realm). I thought HPB believed the former, correctly or incorrectly.

Hi Brother JMG,

This month I will be raised to the grade of Master Mason. I am curious if you know of any good books on esoteric Freemasonry. I came acrodd the books by Jean Louise DiBiassi (head of the Aurum Solid and Freemason) but am not sure if I should look at something else.

replies.title: Re: Esoteric Freemasonry
replies.content: Bro. Ian, glad to hear this. DiBiassi's books are a good place to start. You might also like W.L. Wilmshurst's The Meaning of Masonry.

replies.title: Re: Esoteric Freemasonry
replies.content:
I'm not a fan of DiBiassi. He started his own Egyptian Masonic outfit in Nevada, based on who-knows-what, and promoted it as being acceptable to regular GL Freemasons, even though it had the three Craft degrees, which would have made it Clandestine and gotten any regular GL Mason who joined it charged with a Masonic offense and possibly expelled. I think his Masonic information is suspect, but that's only my opinion.
I second JMG's recommendation of Wilmshurt, and there are two books by him easily available and more that takes a bit more to get to. His Lodge of the Living Stones is still active in the U.K. and offers associate membership. Mat Ravignat has put together an incredibly interesting reconstruction of Egyptian Masonry using original source material from Tschoudy, Cagliostro and Di Sangro. It's easily obtainable on Amazon, if no where else. I also think Piers Vaughn has a couple of interesting books available that go into the more interesting and esoteric aspects of Freemasonry.

comments.title: Acme Brand Blessing
comments.content:
What exactly is a blessing?

I ask because I have noticed myself reflexively blessing and offering blessing for various things and realized I don't really have much behind it. Like I see an ambulance go by and think "bless them" without any specific intention or invoked spiritual force except some vague and unexamined positive notion.

Do such things have any spiritual force at all given the diffuse intent and reflexive nature or is there some universal direction that "blessings" move things and I'm giving power in that direction? Are such things more of an inert sentiment than a magical act?

replies.title: Re: Acme Brand Blessing
replies.content:
A blessing is a positive intention directed at someone or something. It's very much worth doing, even in a reflexive fashion. In fact, doing this is a basic spiritual practice in some traditions:

https://ecosophia.dreamwidth.org/208136.html

You're adding the power of your intention toward things going well, and that benefits you as well as whatever you bless.

replies.title: Re: Acme Brand Blessing
replies.content:
> You're adding the power of your intention toward things going well, and that benefits you as well as whatever you bless.

Is it ever possible that someone’s blessing is better avoided, maybe because it has some secret strings attached or because the person giving the blessing has karmic qualities that are better left alone?

I ask because in the past I knew a woman who, when I first met her, I had instant intuitive alarm bells going off: Here’s Trouble. She had a regular habit of saying things like “Oh bless”, or often “Bless you” rather than a more standard “Thank you”. Over time we both ended up in positions at work where we had to spend significant time together. I don’t doubt her sincerity, I think she wanted to be, and was desperately trying to be, a good person. But her personal life was such a mess of drama, poor communication and broken relationships, no matter how sympathetic I became to her I still felt that instinctive revulsion. And I found myself sometimes secretly trying to dodge or reject any “blessings” aimed at me. Maybe that was the wrong approach, but it was a hard thing to overcome.

replies.title: Re: Acme Brand Blessing
replies.content: No, it's not a mistake. I always teach people always to offer blessings, never to push them on people -- thus the "my blessings to all who will have them" that some people use on this forum, for example. A blessing may or may not be appropriate. But there's also a darker factor. Witches -- meaning by this not Wiccans, but the original meaning of the word -- are people so consumed by negative emotions that they radiate nastiness at everyone they encounter. Very often they make a habit of lavish praise or verbal blessings, sometimes for camouflage and sometimes because they're trying not to be what they are. Those "blessings" are normally followed by a string of disasters. This is one of the many reasons why basic protective magic is so important!

replies.title: Re: Acme Brand Blessing
replies.content:
> sometimes because they're trying not to be what they are

Are witches born as such, and then one can try to reject that innate quality? Or does one choose, intentionally or not, to become a witch, and then can attempt to undo that choice later in life?

replies.title: Re: Acme Brand Blessing
replies.content: It varies. Some people who practice evil magic in one life are born as witches in the next; others wallow in unpleasant emotions so intensively that they achieve the same condition in a single life. Yes, you can always overcome that state, but it takes hard work: you have to fight against the habit of gathering up your anger, your fear, your depression, and your other negative emotions and flinging them at other people -- and that means, of course, that you have to deal with those emotions yourself.

4) if a tree, or a rock, or any object / thing is possessed by dark / nefarious / negative energy / forces / entities is there anything that can be done? (I ask through curiosity as I have had the feeling to avoid certain things people and places, but am almost always drawn to trees no matter how they appear, in these approaches I wonder if there is anything more I can do other than as I do now of recognising the tree by touch - or if even that maybe a no-no)


Thank you for considering these questions from England, all the best

replies.title: Re: Possession
replies.content:
1) There's a difference between possession and obsession. Possession is when another entity takes over your body and shoves you out of it -- that's the full-scale, growly-voice, somebody-else-is-in-that-body sort of thing. It's very rare. Obsession is when another entity gets influence over your thoughts and feelings; the original person remains there, but is subject to an ongoing suction in the direction of whatever thoughts and feelings the intruding energy wants to put there. It's more common.

2) They can have negative effects on people's consciousness, certainly. That's not possession, nor is it really obsession -- it's just the same kind of mental distortion you get from watching too many manipulative adverts on the telly, for example.

3) Contact with positive spiritual beings is an entirely different thing, because they won't try to manipulate you against your will -- only evil spirits do that.

4) It requires very extensive training and practice -- as in, years of hard work -- to do anything significant in a situation like that.

replies.title: Re: Possession
replies.content: Thank you for the response, puts much into perspective & work to do on my discernment

replies.title: Re: Possession
replies.content: It may help you sort this out to know that there are (at least) three distinct sorts of trances. (1) There is the visionary trance, where one's senses expand and one can perceive more than is ordinarily perceptible. (2) There is the possession trance, where one's ordinary "self" goes away, falls asleep, or perhaps even ceases to exist for a while, and some other "self" or being takes over one's body. And (3) there is the journeying trance, where one's surroundings cease to be perceptible, and one instead perceives oneself to be in some other space/time, able to watch and possibly act there instead of in one's every-day space/time.

replies.title: Re: Possession
replies.content: Hmm! That's quite a useful taxonomy; thank you.

replies.title: Re: Possession
replies.content: You're most welcome.

replies.title: Re: Possession
replies.content: Thank you for this - I had not considered referring to possession (or obsession) as types of trance - much to consider here

replies.title: Re: Possession
replies.content:
Possession trances seem to me (as an outsider) to be a common, and greatly valued, occurrence in the rituals of Haitian vodou, as described by Maya Deren. Deren, quite unexpectedly and without her consent, was "ridden" by a lwa herself while observing a ritual there, and so she was able to provide a first-hand description of the experience. She (Maya) simply ceased to exist anywhere, so far as she could tell, during the time that she was being "ridden"; other people present experienced some other being than her, far more powerful than her, using her entranced body for its own purposes of manifestation to the assembled congregation.

Journeying trances, in contrast, seem to be normal in shamanistic rituals, as for example, among the Inuit.

And vision trances are not uncommon in traditional Christianity. Indeed, there is at least one magical grimoire, The Sworn Book of Honorius of Thebes, that describes a ritual to cause the ritualist to experience the full Beatific Vision of Roman Catholic Christianity -- which experience involves all the senses, not sight alone. ("Visionary" is just a convenient shorthand term for this sort of trance; usually more senses than sight alone are involved.)

replies.title: Re: Aphantasia
replies.content:
A few notes from someone who has aphantasia as is doing my best to investigate and work with it:
- It can affect not just visualisation, but all the minds senses (sight, touch, taste, smell, feelings) and when all of those are absent (as for me) it is sometimes termed full or complete aphantasia.
- My investigation and practices so far indicate it is strongly connected to a significant trauma in the subtle body, and that healing or addressing the trauma can remove the restrictions preventing visualisation. I have faint indications of why and how that may have happened for me (not in this life), but no definitive understanding as yet.
- What I can say of is having had some minor success with various techniques, I am absolutely sure it is not just a matter of terminology or a semantic mis-understanding as is a common criticism. The effect (or absence of effect) is both real, and also amenable to treatment (which is highly controversial to say - many who have aphanstasia resent being labelled as lacking).

If I'm successful in 'curing' my condition I have an aspiration to write a book on my experiences. I do see the world in quite a different fashion to others, which has many benefits but as you note makes many traditional spiritual teachings challenging to work with, if not entirely inaccessible.

comments.title: Gnosticism
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

What are your thoughts on Gnosticism - ie. that we live in a cosmic prison/factory farm designed to cause us suffering either for entertainment or as a food source?

Were they onto something, or just 1st century goths/emos, wallowing in despair?

To me, the universe makes more sense as being run by an incompetent or lazy creator, rather than an outright malicious one. Which would fit in with the idea of Dion Fortune's Solar Logos, who is having to learn "on the job" and has been promoted into incompetence:

https://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/Peter_principle

replies.title: Re: Gnosticism
replies.content:
That's only one kind of Gnosticism, and to my mind, yeah, it's the emo sort. "The universe isn't the way I want it! The Creator must be BAAAAD!!!"

To my mind the universe is what it is because our comfort and convenience aren't that important in the great scheme of things. The Solar Logos has much more important things to contemplate than whether you or I like what he's doing. Given the huge amount of human suffering that people inflict on themselves, and the equally huge amount that they inflict on each other, he doubtless rolled his incandescent eyes and said, "Well, okay, I guess they like to be miserable" and turned away to get to work dealing with an incoming comet swarm or something.

replies.title: Gods and other Powers, and humans (was Re: Gnosticism)
replies.content: Given that Powers such as the Solar Logos really don't concern themselves much with human beings, why do some of them become more involved with our species?  Some deities and spirits seem very interested in us, or at least in certain (favorite) people.

replies.title: Re: Gods and other Powers, and humans (was Re: Gnosticism)
replies.content: Some of us keep goldfish or feed birds. Others train dogs to hunt. I figure it's similar with them.

replies.title: Re: Gnosticism
replies.content:
(OP)
Haha, yeah, that makes sense.

I'm just imagining Yaldabaoth on his first day, desperately trying to make the everything in the whole universe run smoothly, then giving up a week later and just making sure that not too many things are on fire. Like most jobs, really.

On a podcast I listened to the other week, one of the guests said: "if humans really wanted peace, there would be peace". There was a lot of truth in that statement.

replies.title: Re: Gnosticism
replies.content:
This may or may not make me less popular around here, but I do not believe that kind of Gnosticism is so easily dismissed as goth-emos whose positions can be reduced to something like "The universe isn't the way I want it! The Creator must be BAAAD!!!" I've spent a lot of time reading, studying, and meditating upon the documents of the Nag Hammadi library, as well as Manichaean works. They might, in the end, be wrong, but they're not simply motivated by such a simplistic emo-goth mentality. That'd be no more accurate than dismissing Druidry as sentimentalist tree huggers or Christianity as Puritanical sex shaming.

To my mind, it boils down to this: whether something like world-weary Gnosticism is true is a matter of perspective. Looked at in a certain way, the Solar Logos seeing human misery, concluding we like it, and going about other matters can seem justifiable to one person and is no problem for them. Fine. There must be plenty of gods and spiritual beings that one can work with, and goals to pursue, with that worldview. But another might see how such a Solar Logos is much like the Demiurge of the Valentinian Gnostic myth: an imperfect, not evil, creator who has done the best he can given his particular nature. The option for this person is to seek out a deity or power that offers a different spiritual path: Valentinian Gnosticism, maybe Theravada Buddhism, etc.

replies.title: Re: Gnosticism
replies.content:
I'd point out that most Druids, including me, are in fact sentimental about nature and hug trees on occasion, so it's not wrong, just a little simplistic, to call them sentimental tree huggers. In the same way, those who condemn the universe and its creator or creators because it doesn't live up to their expectations are demonstrating the kind of mentality I've described, and it may well be overly simplistic to sum them up that way -- but it's not wrong.

I've also studied the Nag Hammadi scriptures and a great deal of other Gnostic and para-Gnostic literature, by the way; as a bishop in the Universal Gnostic Church -- part of the branch of the movement that doesn't embrace that attitude -- it was part of my training. You're certainly free to disagree with me, of course, but to me the world-hating end of Gnosticism has always seemed afflicted with a badly overinflated sense of entitlement. But then I've also studied Plotinus's critique of that kind of Gnosticism, and more or less agree with him. Here again, your mileage may vary.

replies.title: Re: Gnosticism
replies.content: All fair enough, but I tend to avoid simplistic summations since, in my experience, they tend to eventually lead others to only consider the summation, without any consideration of nuances. I am not, to be sure, saying you're guilty of that. That said, I have also read Against the Gnostics, and a lot of criticisms of Gnosticism by the early Church fathers. And I've read criticisms of the criticisms. I do not consider myself a world-weary type (I, in fact, look forward to reincarnating into a future where climate change has made much of the Earth a humid jungle planet). But I do think a huge part of this is a matter of perspective, and not inherent and objective truths about the reality of the world and its creator(s). The world is full of different spiritual paths, many with diametrically opposed goals. The world-weary paths might be a valid option (that is, can actually achieve their actual salvific goals) for some folks, and I don't find that problematic.

replies.title: Re: Gnosticism
replies.content: Oh, granted, and it may just be that the world-hating approach grates on the nerves of a tree-hugging nature worshipper like me!

comments.title: Sphere of Protection – Opening and Closing
comments.content:
I’m a newbie in esoteric matters, and for the past six months or so I’ve been learning and practicing the Sphere of Protection, following along JMGs instructions. Since this is my only spiritual practice, I’ve been giving it a lot of attention as I slowly learn and integrate it into my life. (Up until this point, I’ve made a policy of flying well under the radar of gods and such, staying respectful but very low key.)

I’d like to post some questions on each part of the Sphere ritual over the next few weeks, starting with the Opening and Closing, which I think of as a single process that bookends the Callings.

One of the things that struck me as I do the opening, is that it reenacts a creation myth: The sky god mating with the earth goddess, then giving birth to human spirit. At least this is what I feel when I follow the white light’s pathway. Is this how you interpret it, and does it have other meanings for you as well as, or instead of, this reenactment?

At the other end, in the Closing, I come back to noticing the white light again, and find it strengthened and reinforced by the spectrum of colours in the Callings. (This fits nicely with my simplistic understanding of physics, where white light is the additive product of the full visible spectrum).

The added colours of the Callings seem to boost the pure white light so that it is strong enough to cast the Sphere around myself, my wife, our four cats, and our little home and garden. For me, the Sphere is a real sphere in shape, large enough to enclose our city lot, that descends down into the earth and up into the air placing us in the centre of the sphere. It spills over into our immediate neighbours yards and homes a bit, which I always feel is good for neighbourly relations.

Does this describe the Closing for you as well, or are there parts that are important to you that I am missing or misunderstanding?

In terms of the wording, my memory is not great these days, so I keep the main request at both the opening and closing pretty simple: "May the Spirits bless and [renew|protect] myself and all that I love, today and all days". (Renew is for the opening, and Protect is for the closing)

I’ll be very pleased to hear anything you have to say about the opening and closing, and what they mean for you in your own practice!

replies.title: Re: Sphere of Protection – Opening and Closing
replies.content: What I'm going to say will doubtless annoy the bejesus out of you, but it's what John Gilbert said do me when I came to him with similar questions:  "Good. Keep going and see what else it has to reveal to you." The point is that there's always a deeply personal dimension to each practitioner's experience of this (and in fact every) ritual, and it's important to let that teach you, rather than seeking validation from somebody else.

comments.title: SOP Gone Wrong
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

Over the last few months I've been attempting to learn the Sphere of Protection, after many years of working with pentagram rituals. I had various reasons for this. But lately I've run into serious difficulties, and I'm hoping you can help me figure out the best way forward.

First, I want to make it clear that I tried to proceed as carefully as possible. I discontinued the pentagram rituals for a month or so, and then began the simplest form of the SOP that I could, based on the version you posted at this blog. The powers I invoked for this form of the SOP were the Christian trinity. I did not proceed beyond the circulation of the light.

This worked well for about three months. In time I began to experiment with working with simple qigong and taijiquan forms within the sphere, again with very good results. After a month or two, I began a slightly more elaborate version of the SOP by incorporating the Solar Plexus center, as described in the Dolmen Arch: Prior to this I had formed the Sphere at the heart center. Again, there were no problems, and indeed the use of the Solar Plexus seemed to greatly strengthen the sphere itself.

After a month of this, I decided to attempt to work with the elements. I began with Air, using the names YHVH and the archangel Raphael. I've worked with the elements extensively and the powers of Air felt like the powers of Air invoked by any other means. I continued to experiment with taijiquan forms in the protected space, again with no problems.

Then, about 10 days ago, I moved on to Water. The names I used were EL and the archangel Gabriel. The first time I attempted to invoke Water, I drew the symbol incorrectly, and got only a minimal response, though a sense of Water being present. The second time I did it correctly and got a very strong response-- much stronger, in fact, than I usually get when working with Water in a pentagram ritual, or through pore breathing. I did not follow the SOP with any movement practice that day. I specifically asked for protection from harmful or unbalancing forms of Water during the banishing portion.

About two hours later, I was in line for an indoor water park with my family, and I was suddenly overwhelmed with pain in my gut. I had to make a beeline for the nearest-- crowded, loud, unsanitary, public-- bathroom, where I spent the rest of the day. I never made it into the water park, which was probably just as well, because it was as crowded, loud and unsanitary as its attached restroom, and everyone had a completely miesrable experience.

My trouble didn't end there, though. I spent the rest of the week laid up with constant pain in my gut, unable to consume anything except for liquid (water) foods. My wife was able to be home that week, fortunately, and so able to help care for me (a change in our usual relationship dynamic, a watery matter as well), while I laid helpless on the couch, unable to go to work (I dislike my job intensely, raising the watery issues of emotions and relationships).

Now it's 10 days later, and I'm still not well. I've been to a doctor, and when I went back I was sent to an ER, but no normal medical tests show anything wrong. This is, emphatically, NOT a request for medical advice: I include this for completeness, not as a request for diagnosis or treatment. One friend has sent me distance healing via a Rose Cross ritual, having been given the go-ahead by divination. The first time, he experienced a sensation of intense wet and cold in his own body; after the second time, he found himself laid up all night with symptoms very much like my own, and is still recovering. Another friend who attempted to help via the same ritual also came down with a serious illness, though of a respiratory and not a digestive nature.

All that said, my questions are,

Given the foregoing, how would you proceed? Would it be worth it to continue with the Sphere, in order to see if continued practice balanced the elemental energies out? Divination did not recommend this; a geomantic chart had Amissio as both witnesses and Populus as judge, but my personal divinations sometimes go awry.

In your view, does it seem more likely that the SOP simply went wrong, or is unsuited for me for some reason? Or may it have been the result of hostile magic, or of performing magic during Eclipse Season? Mercury Retrogrades tend to affect me very strongly (Mercury is extremely well-dignified in my natal chart), but the retrograde hadn't begun yet the day I did the Water working.

Do you have any other thoughts?

Thank you in advance.

replies.title: Re: SOP Gone Wrong
replies.content: I would encourage you to stop the elemental side of the workings for the time being and just work with the opening and closing for a while. Continue doing that for six months or so. At that point, if divinations approve, try adding the elemental workings again, giving a month to air before going on to water. If that works without trouble, you're good. If it doesn't, the SoP is not something you should be doing, and you'll need to go back to the LBRP.

replies.title: Re: SOP Gone Wrong
replies.content: Thank you very much for the advice.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) What you're calling the SIRP, I was taught to call the GIRP; the versions that invoke the individual elements alone are derived from it. It's equivalent to Crowley's version, again, because he borrowed his version from the Golden Dawn.

2) In the worldview of Renaissance magic, there are three broad levels of existence -- the elemental level, the astral level, and the spiritual level. These are subdivided into planes in more modern works, but the basic division as given by Cornelius Agrippa et al. is still relevant. Matural magic works entirely on the elemental level and is limited by its conditions; astrological magic works on the astral level, and so doesn't have to worry about conditions on the elemental level; what Agrippa calls spiritual magic works on the spiritual level, and thus doesn't have to worry about astrological or material conditions. The downside is that you have to elevate your consciousness to the spiritual level to work that kind of magic.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I recommend S[amuel] Foster Damon, William Blake: His Philosophy and Symbols (1924). Damon was a gifted poet, an occultist, and a pioneering Blake scholar back in the days when Blake was generally regarded in academia as a madman and not a fit subject for serious scholarship. The book is out of copyright now and freely available online. He also wrote A Blake Dictionary (1965).

Damon was a very unconventional academic, a faculty member of Brown University's English Department. He contributed to Rider's Occult Review during the 1920s, reviewed books on magic by A. E. Waite and Lynn Thorndike, and argued in print with the occultist and alchemist R. Watson Counsell (Sax Rohmer's occult mentor). I never met the man, alas! He was seriously ill when I came to Brown in 1967, and died in 1971. His papers are preserved in Brown University's Special Collections Library.

There's a nice appreciation of the man by his great-nephew, P. D. Littlefield, available online at: https://static1.squarespace.com/static/5e35e50ee72e4563b10feea7/t/5f08d2f295c33e741e9e29ee/1594413815609/Damon+presentation.pdf

comments.title: OCD
comments.content:
JMG - do you have any recommendations for overcoming and/or dealing with extreme OCD tendencies?

I've always been very cautious and detail-oriented overall. When I was a kid, I remember sometimes going through short, intermittent phases where I'd have to switch the light on and off several times until it felt just right, or I'd have to clean my room "perfectly". It was never that big of a deal.

Then, when I was about 27 (20 years ago), out of the blue, I started experiencing a lot of more serious and lasting OCD symptoms. Originally, they were mostly around ensuring the accuracy of things like spreadsheets, emails that I wrote, checks that I'd write, etc.

Over the past 20 years, things have gradually and consistently gotten more severe. Writing emails and texts take forever, as I feel the need to re-read them sometimes dozens of times. Same thing with writing down pretty much anything or any sort of data entry. I also spend a lot of time checking and re-checking the arrangements of things - simply putting something in a drawer leads to several or dozens of rounds of checking the drawer to make sure the thing is in there and nothing else was disturbed, leaving a room leads to several scans of the room to make sure nothing was left out of place, etc.

My rational mind understands the absurdity, and I never find mistakes. But, the doubt and anxiety I experience in the moment seems impossible to overcome. At times, I force myself to just walk away and trust, but the doubt is so nagging, that I'm unable to focus on anything else until I go back and satisfactorily convince myself that things are correct. It's reached a point where it's materially affecting my mood and effectiveness as a person. I tried some rational-behavioral therapy about 15 years ago, and it didn't provide any help.

Thanks!

replies.title: Re: OCD
replies.content:
Not JMG, and not a doctor, but feel moved to share that many decades ago I suffered from OCD for about 6 months and it came directly after a bout of strep throat. I did not connect the dots... but it sure was weird at the time. I never could perceive any psychological reason for the OCD; just, after I recovered from strep, very all of a sudden I needed to count things, and check things, and so on. The OCD faded away eventually. A few years ago I read somewhere that it turns out that strep throat can cause problems in the brain, a specific sort of inflammation, that, after the strep is gone, can cause OCD to manifest-- my childhood case sounded like something quite typical!! I was astounded to read that, and to me, it made perfect sense.

So if I were in your shoes with persistent OCD I migth start looking into whether there might be some anti-inflammatories that could help.

One anti-inflammatory that I take just for general health purposes is fennel tea or ginger tea with a little bit of raw honey. I find that very very soothing generally.

Again I am not doctor, this is not medical advice, just sharing.

I myself wiill be very interested in JMG's response to your question.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hi John,

How does GD magic differ from astrological magic as a path of spiritual development? I have it in my mind that the GD system is a mix of practical getting-stuff-done magic
while training yourself spiritually (not mutually exclusive I know)...where as the magic of something like the Picatrix is mostly creating talismans to contact and bring the influence of celestial spirits into one's life. I'm sure some insights would gained from such a practice, but it seems more in the making-stuff-happen vein without changing yourself too much. Can you set me straight on this?

The person that keeps asking about grimoires and pentacles earlier in this Magic Monday..he wants to make make the miraculous magic happen in his/her life, it's what still makes me excited about the Picatrix. The further along I travel I see much more that as you have replied to me before the big secret of magic is changing yourself.

2) Without getting specific, when would you reach for the Picatrix as opposed to GD magic?

Thank you for Magic Monday.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) The GD system is primarily a system of spiritual development. The practical applications are meant to assist that process. The Picatrix assumes that you're already pursuing your spiritual development through the standard methods of Neoplatonic spirituality; it's as though somebody published the GD adeptus minor papers apart from the rest of the system. You have to go to the later grimoires to get magic that's purely the get-stuff-happen variety -- even the earlier grimoires assume that you're using standard Christian practices for your spiritual development, which is why they talk about attending Mass, fasting, alms, and so on.

1a) Wanting to change the world without changing yourself is the classic trap of the novice mage. It's so very tempting to think that you can bully the cosmos into handing you what you want! It can take any amount of time -- whole lives, for example -- to figure out that "what you want" is usually a self-contradictory mess of automatic cravings picked up from family, friends, and mass media, which will make a mess of your life rather than bringing you the happiness you think they'll provide. That's when the door opens and the real work begins.

2) When I don't want to have to put much energy into the work. Calculating an election is easy; rising on the planes to the realms where celestial magic becomes possible is not.

comments.title: Planes of reality
comments.content:
JMG, another set of questions if you don't mind:

1) How do I "progress" to a higher plane after death and not return to earth? Since there are bad elements on the higher planes, I assume being "good" has nothing to do with it?

2) The Hindus and Buddhists, from what I understand, believe that a soul more or less just rises to the godly planes or descends to the hellish planes based on good and bad karma. That being said, the human plane is unique because it is most conducive to attaining enlightenment and thus escaping the entire wheel of cycles. I am curious if you agree with the enlightenment in the human plane as an "escape" of the wheel; if so, how does one attain to enlightenment?

replies.title: Re: Planes of reality
replies.content:
1) That happens when you've learned the lessons you need to learn in human incarnation, and work through any remaining karma you might have. You can't make it happen, you simply have to allow the ripening process to continue.

2) Some Hindus and some Buddhists teach that -- both are very rich, complex faiths with many options, and there are some of each that recognize that this world of illusion is itself identical to nirvana, that the wheel of illusion is itself an illusion, and so is escape from it and that -- as Sri Aurobindo put it -- in a very real sense, the Absolute is simply an eternal child playing an eternal game in an eternal garden. That's my view. It's all lila, a dance in which we take part, and there's no need to be in any hurry to go sit down like a wallflower and watch the others dance!

replies.title: Re: Planes of reality
replies.content: OK, so how do I figure out what lessons I am supposed to learn in this life?

replies.title: Re: Planes of reality
replies.content:
You don't figure them out. You live your life, and the experiences of your life teach you what you need to know.

One of the problems with New Age ideologies is that they make all this sound like going through a buffet line and picking out experiences. It's not. It's much more like the way a fetus is formed in the womb -- its pattern of growth is not something over which the fetus has any choice. Only very, very late in the process do you begin to get some sense of where you're going and why, and by then you're probably on your last life anyway.
